prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
When Eli volunteered at Mr. Ko’s clock shop, he expected to polish brass cases and wrap repaired clocks in paper for customers. Instead, Mr. Ko led him to a workbench after closing and set three clocks in a row beneath a lamp.
“Listen,” he said.
Eli listened. The clocks all seemed to say the same thing: tick, tick, tick. One had a dark walnut case, one gleamed with a glass door, and the third had chipped paint around its face. To Eli, they looked more different than they sounded. But Mr. Ko frowned and bent closer. “This one hurries,” he said, touching the smallest clock. “And that one drifts.”
He loosened a screw and lowered a pendulum weight by what seemed like no distance at all. Then he waited again. Eli tried to hear the change and failed. Yet the next morning, when the repaired schoolhouse clock chimed noon exactly as students poured onto the sidewalk, Mr. Ko smiled as if a secret had finally snapped into place. Eli understood then that accuracy did not come from the polished wood or the gold numbers. It came from patient adjustments so small most people would never notice them.
Which statement best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Customers care more about a clock’s appearance than its accuracy.
choices.B: Careful, nearly invisible adjustments can determine whether something works well.
choices.C: Eli decides that clocks are more interesting than he had expected.
choices.D: Mr. Ko prefers working alone after the shop has closed.

explanation: Eli learns that the most important part of repair is the tiny adjustment that makes the clock accurate. The main trap is “Eli decides that clocks are more interesting than he had expected.” because that is true but less central than the idea about hidden precision.

Eli understood then that accuracy did not come from the polished wood or the gold numbers. It came from patient adjustments so small most people would never notice them.
What can the reader most reasonably infer about Mr. Ko?

choices.A: He believes appearance matters more than precision.
choices.B: He thinks reliable craftsmanship depends on close attention to detail.
choices.C: He wants Eli to avoid learning the most difficult parts of the job.
choices.D: He dislikes repairing clocks that belong to public buildings.

explanation: Mr. Ko notices tiny timing differences and corrects them carefully. The main trap is “He believes appearance matters more than precision.” because the passage shows the opposite.

prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
Some cities are building tree trenches beneath sidewalks to help manage stormwater and improve conditions for street trees. A tree trench is a connected underground area of soil and stone that can collect rainwater running off nearby pavement. Instead of sending all that water directly into storm drains, the trench allows some of it to soak into the ground, where tree roots can use it.
These systems can help reduce flooding and support healthier trees, but they are not simple to design. Engineers must consider soil conditions, underground utilities, and how much water is likely to enter the trench during a storm. If the trench is too shallow or poorly placed, it may fill too quickly or fail to support the trees above it. For that reason, tree trenches work best when they are planned to fit the needs of a particular street rather than copied from one site to another without changes.
Which statement best summarizes the passage?

choices.A: Tree trenches can help with stormwater and tree health, but they must be designed for local conditions.
choices.B: Tree trenches are mainly decorative spaces used to make sidewalks look greener.
choices.C: Tree trenches can replace every kind of storm drain in a city.
choices.D: Tree trenches work best when the same design is used on every block.

explanation: The passage emphasizes both their benefits and the need for site-specific planning. The main trap is “Tree trenches can replace every kind of storm drain in a city.” because the author never makes that absolute claim.

choices.B: to argue that sidewalks should be removed from city streets
choices.C: to compare several species of street trees
choices.D: to describe the history of urban stonework

explanation: The author defines the system and explains why its success depends on conditions underground and at the street. The main trap is “to compare several species of street trees” because the passage is not mainly about tree types.

prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
Ruby signed up to help with the school play because she thought she would be painting backdrops or handing actors their props. Instead, Mr. Keller brought her to the fly system above the stage, where ropes ran through pulleys and disappeared into the ceiling shadows.
“We check balance first,” he said.
Ruby stared at the sandbags hanging from hooks along the wall. Across the stage, other students were brushing bright clouds onto a cardboard skyline, laughing whenever someone dropped a paint tray. Up in the rigging loft, the work felt slow and quiet. Mr. Keller clipped a weight onto one line, tugged the rope, frowned, and shifted the bag again. “Too light,” he said. “Now too heavy.” To Ruby, the difference between the two tries seemed too small to matter.
But that evening, when the curtain rose on the rooftop scene, the painted skyline floated down in one smooth motion and stopped exactly where it should. Nothing lurched. Nothing tilted. From the audience, the movement seemed effortless, as if scenery simply knew where to go. Ruby smiled in the dark, understanding at last that the calm beauty of the scene had depended on people who had measured, adjusted, and tested things long before the audience sat down.
Which statement best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Ruby learns that careful technical preparation can make stage effects appear smooth and natural.
choices.B: Backdrop painting is more creative than stage rigging.
choices.C: School plays are harder to produce than most students realize.
choices.D: Mr. Keller wants the audience to notice the ropes above the stage.

explanation: Ruby realizes the effortless motion of the scenery depends on hidden balancing work. The main trap is “School plays are harder to produce than most students realize.” because that is broader and less precise than the main idea.

prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
Some cities are testing pedestrian scrambles at especially busy intersections. In a pedestrian scramble, all vehicle traffic stops at once, allowing people to cross in every direction, including diagonally, during a special walking phase. Supporters say this can reduce conflicts between turning cars and pedestrians because drivers and walkers are separated in time rather than trying to move through the intersection at once.
However, pedestrian scrambles are not equally useful everywhere. Their success depends on factors such as traffic volume, signal timing, the number of people crossing, and the layout of the streets. At some intersections, a scramble may improve safety and flow. At others, it may create longer waits without solving the main problem. For that reason, transportation planners usually study each site carefully before deciding whether this design is the right fit.
Which statement best summarizes the passage?

choices.A: Pedestrian scrambles are always the fastest way to move people through an intersection.
choices.B: Pedestrian scrambles can improve safety in some places, but they work best when chosen for the right intersection.
choices.C: Drivers and pedestrians should never share the same streets.
choices.D: Diagonal crossings are the only important feature of city traffic design.

explanation: The passage explains both the possible benefit and the importance of careful site selection. The main trap is “Pedestrian scrambles are always the fastest way to move people through an intersection.” because the author says they are not equally useful everywhere.

choices.A: install the scramble without gathering any information because all busy intersections need one

prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
Tariq volunteered for the school robotics showcase because he expected to explain inventions to visitors and demonstrate how the machines moved. Instead, Ms. Lin led him to a table near the back wall, where several robots stood motionless beside a tangle of cords and charging banks.
“We check batteries first,” she said.
Tariq tried not to look disappointed. Across the room, other volunteers practiced introducing the teams and rehearsed when to press the start buttons. At his table, Ms. Lin plugged in one machine, waited, unplugged it, and then marked a number on a clipboard. “Still weak,” she said. A second battery pack looked identical but held a charge longer. To Tariq, the process seemed repetitive and dull.
But during the afternoon demonstration, he saw why Ms. Lin had insisted on it. One robot completed its maze without slowing. Another arm lifted its blocks in a smooth series of turns instead of stopping halfway through a motion. Nothing stalled. Nothing sputtered. The audience only saw machines that seemed ready and confident, as if they had awakened that way on their own. Tariq, however, now knew that steady performance had begun hours earlier, with testing that few people would ever notice.
Which statement best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Tariq learns that dependable performance often depends on careful preparation that stays out of sight.
choices.B: Robotics demonstrations are more popular than Tariq expected.
choices.C: Ms. Lin prefers charts and clipboards to actual machines.
choices.D: Battery packs are the most expensive part of a robot.

explanation: Tariq realizes that battery testing makes the public demonstration seem smooth and confident. The main trap is “Ms. Lin prefers charts and clipboards to actual machines.” because the clipboard is only part of the testing process, not the point of the passage.

choices.A: She believes equipment should be checked carefully before it is asked to perform.
choices.B: She wants Tariq to stay away from the visitors.
choices.C: She thinks the robots are too simple to fail.
choices.D: She prefers demonstration days to preparation days.

explanation: Ms. Lin insists on checking batteries because she wants the robots to work steadily later. The main trap is “She thinks the robots are too simple to fail.” because her actions show she expects problems if preparation is careless.

prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
Tariq volunteered for the school robotics showcase because he expected to explain inventions to visitors and demonstrate how the machines moved. Instead, Ms. Lin led him to a table near the back wall, where several robots stood motionless beside a tangle of cords and charging banks.
“We check batteries first,” she said.
Tariq tried not to look disappointed. Across the room, other volunteers practiced introducing the teams and rehearsed when to press the start buttons. At his table, Ms. Lin plugged in one machine, waited, unplugged it, and then marked a number on a clipboard. “Still weak,” she said. A second battery pack looked identical but held a charge longer. To Tariq, the process seemed repetitive and dull.
But during the afternoon demonstration, he saw why Ms. Lin had insisted on it. One robot completed its maze without slowing. Another arm lifted its blocks in a smooth series of turns instead of stopping halfway through a motion. Nothing stalled. Nothing sputtered. The audience only saw machines that seemed ready and confident, as if they had awakened that way on their own. Tariq, however, now knew that steady performance had begun hours earlier, with testing that few people would ever notice.
How does Tariq’s attitude change over the course of the passage?

choices.A: from doubtful to impressed
choices.B: from excited to angry
choices.C: from frightened to calm
choices.D: from proud to embarrassed

explanation: Tariq starts by seeing the task as dull but ends up admiring its importance. The main trap is “from frightened to calm” because fear is not a major part of the passage.

prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
Tariq volunteered for the school robotics showcase because he expected to explain inventions to visitors and demonstrate how the machines moved. Instead, Ms. Lin led him to a table near the back wall, where several robots stood motionless beside a tangle of cords and charging banks.
“We check batteries first,” she said.
Tariq tried not to look disappointed. Across the room, other volunteers practiced introducing the teams and rehearsed when to press the start buttons. At his table, Ms. Lin plugged in one machine, waited, unplugged it, and then marked a number on a clipboard. “Still weak,” she said. A second battery pack looked identical but held a charge longer. To Tariq, the process seemed repetitive and dull.
But during the afternoon demonstration, he saw why Ms. Lin had insisted on it. One robot completed its maze without slowing. Another arm lifted its blocks in a smooth series of turns instead of stopping halfway through a motion. Nothing stalled. Nothing sputtered. The audience only saw machines that seemed ready and confident, as if they had awakened that way on their own. Tariq, however, now knew that steady performance had begun hours earlier, with testing that few people would ever notice.
As used in the passage, the word “stalled” most nearly means

choices.A: became damaged permanently
choices.B: stopped moving forward
choices.C: turned too quickly
choices.D: made a loud sound

explanation: In context, a stalled robot would stop in the middle of its task. The main trap is “became damaged permanently” because stalling refers to interruption, not necessarily permanent damage.

prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
Tariq volunteered for the school robotics showcase because he expected to explain inventions to visitors and demonstrate how the machines moved. Instead, Ms. Lin led him to a table near the back wall, where several robots stood motionless beside a tangle of cords and charging banks.
“We check batteries first,” she said.
Tariq tried not to look disappointed. Across the room, other volunteers practiced introducing the teams and rehearsed when to press the start buttons. At his table, Ms. Lin plugged in one machine, waited, unplugged it, and then marked a number on a clipboard. “Still weak,” she said. A second battery pack looked identical but held a charge longer. To Tariq, the process seemed repetitive and dull.
But during the afternoon demonstration, he saw why Ms. Lin had insisted on it. One robot completed its maze without slowing. Another arm lifted its blocks in a smooth series of turns instead of stopping halfway through a motion. Nothing stalled. Nothing sputtered. The audience only saw machines that seemed ready and confident, as if they had awakened that way on their own. Tariq, however, now knew that steady performance had begun hours earlier, with testing that few people would ever notice.
Which detail best supports the idea that the preparation made the demonstration more successful?

choices.A: Other volunteers practiced introducing the teams.
choices.B: A second battery pack looked identical but held a charge longer.
choices.C: One robot completed its maze without slowing, and another arm lifted its blocks in a smooth series of turns.
choices.D: Several robots stood motionless near the back wall.

explanation: This detail shows the successful result of the careful battery testing. The main trap is “A second battery pack looked identical but held a charge longer.” because it hints at the cause but does not show the demonstration’s improved performance as directly.

prompt:
The student council is planning a welcome fair for new students. Members listed the booths they wanted to include they also asked current students which activities had helped them feel comfortable during their first week.
Which revision best corrects the underlined portion?

choices.A: Members listed the booths they wanted to include, they also asked current students which activities had helped them feel comfortable during their first week.
choices.B: Members listed the booths they wanted to include and also asked current students which activities had helped them feel comfortable during their first week.
choices.C: Members listed the booths they wanted to include; also asking current students which activities had helped them feel comfortable during their first week.
choices.D: Members listed the booths they wanted to include because also asked current students which activities had helped them feel comfortable during their first week.

explanation: This revision correctly joins the two actions without creating a comma splice. The main trap is “Members listed the booths they wanted to include, they also asked current students which activities had helped them feel comfortable during their first week.” because it incorrectly joins two complete thoughts with only a comma.

prompt:
Before the final map was printed, each volunteer on the planning team were asked to check the room numbers.
Which revision best corrects the underlined portion?

choices.A: each volunteer on the planning team was asked to check the room numbers
choices.B: each volunteer on the planning team were being asked to check the room numbers
choices.C: each volunteer on the planning team were asked to check the room numbers
choices.D: each volunteer on the planning team ask to check the room numbers

explanation: The subject is singular because “each volunteer” is singular, so “was” is correct. The main trap is “each volunteer on the planning team were asked to check the room numbers” because the plural verb does not agree with the subject.

prompt:
The first draft of the event schedule included too many activities for one afternoon. As a result, the council shortened the program and added more time for conversation.
Which revision of the underlined transition best fits the relationship between the two sentences?

choices.A: As a result
choices.B: For example
choices.C: Likewise
choices.D: Meanwhile

explanation: The second sentence gives the consequence of the first sentence’s problem. The main trap is “For example” because the second sentence is not an illustration; it is an outcome.

prompt: The student council is planning a welcome fair for new students. Members listed the booths they wanted to include and also asked current students which activities had helped them feel comfortable during their first week. Before the final map was printed, each volunteer on the planning team was asked to check the room numbers. The first draft of the event schedule included too many activities for one afternoon. As a result, the council shortened the program and added more time for conversation. [Which sentence would best conclude this paragraph?]

choices.A: School events can be held in many different rooms.
choices.B: Now the council feels more confident that the fair will be friendly, clear, and easy for new students to follow.
choices.C: Printed maps are often folded in different ways.
choices.D: Some students prefer activities that take place outdoors.

explanation: This sentence best concludes the paragraph by tying the edits to the event’s purpose. The main trap is “School events can be held in many different rooms.” because it is too general and does not conclude the idea.

prompt:
The orchestra stepped onto the stage for the evening concert. Holding a silver trumpet, the opening solo was played by Marco near the center microphone.
Which revision best corrects the underlined portion?

choices.A: Holding a silver trumpet, Marco played the opening solo near the center microphone.
choices.B: The opening solo, holding a silver trumpet, was played by Marco near the center microphone.
choices.C: Holding a silver trumpet, the center microphone carried Marco’s opening solo.
choices.D: Marco, the opening solo was played near the center microphone holding a silver trumpet.

explanation: This revision fixes the dangling modifier by placing Marco directly after the phrase about holding the trumpet. The main trap is “The opening solo, holding a silver trumpet, was played by Marco near the center microphone.” because the solo cannot hold the instrument.

prompt:
Families attending the presentation should check in at the welcome table before entering the gym. They should also silence phones before the program begins.
Which revision best clarifies the underlined sentence?

choices.A: Families attending the presentation should also silence phones before the program begins.
choices.B: Phones should also silence before the program begins.
choices.C: It should also silence phones before the program begins.
choices.D: They should also do that before the program begins.

explanation: This version clearly identifies who should do the silencing. The main trap is “They should also do that before the program begins.” because “do that” is vague.

prompt:
(1) The local park will host a free evening walk on neighborhood trees this Friday. (2) Participants will learn how to identify bark patterns, leaf shapes, and seed pods. (3) The walk begins at 6:30 p.m. near the south gate. (4) Registration is recommended because space is limited.
The writer wants to add the following sentence:
Children should attend with an adult.
Where should this sentence be placed to make the paragraph most effective?

choices.A: After sentence 1.
choices.B: After sentence 2.
choices.C: After sentence 3.
choices.D: After sentence 4.

explanation: The information about children attending with an adult should appear immediately after the event is introduced, where readers will notice it early. The main trap is “After sentence 4.” because that would bury an important participation detail too late.

prompt:
Please bring three items a notebook, comfortable shoes, and a water bottle for the walk.
Which revision best corrects the punctuation in the underlined portion?

choices.A: items, a notebook, comfortable shoes, and a water bottle
choices.B: items: a notebook, comfortable shoes, and a water bottle
choices.C: items; a notebook, comfortable shoes, and a water bottle
choices.D: items a notebook, comfortable shoes, and a water bottle

explanation: A colon correctly introduces a list after a complete idea. The main trap is “items, a notebook, comfortable shoes, and a water bottle” because a comma is not sufficient for this introduction.

prompt:
Last spring our class visited the city archives. One historian explain how maps, letters, and photographs are preserved for future researchers.
Which revision best corrects the underlined word?

choices.A: explains
choices.B: explained
choices.C: explaining
choices.D: has explain

explanation: “Last spring” requires a past-tense verb. The main trap is “explains” because it shifts the sentence into present tense.

prompt:
The arctic tern a remarkable traveler between polar regions completes one of the longest migrations in the animal world.
Which revision best punctuates the underlined portion?

choices.A: The arctic tern, a remarkable traveler between polar regions, completes one of the longest migrations in the animal world.
choices.B: The arctic tern a remarkable traveler between polar regions completes one of the longest migrations in the animal world.
choices.C: The arctic tern, a remarkable traveler between polar regions completes, one of the longest migrations in the animal world.
choices.D: The arctic tern a remarkable traveler between polar regions, completes one of the longest migrations in the animal world.

explanation: The phrase describing the tern is nonessential and should be set off with commas. The main trap is “The arctic tern a remarkable traveler between polar regions completes one of the longest migrations in the animal world.” because it omits the needed punctuation.

prompt:
During especially dry months, succulents can do water in their thick leaves for later use.
Which word best replaces the underlined word?

choices.A: store
choices.B: erase
choices.C: flatten
choices.D: spill

explanation: Succulents keep water in their leaves for later use, so “store” is the best replacement. The main trap is “spill” because it means the opposite of what the sentence needs.

prompt:
Original sentences:
The train reached the station late. The students still arrived at the debate tournament on time.
Which revision combines the sentences most effectively?

choices.A: Although the train reached the station late, the students still arrived at the debate tournament on time.
choices.B: The train reached the station late, the students still arrived at the debate tournament on time.
choices.C: The students still arrived at the debate tournament on time, the train reached the station late.
choices.D: Reached the station late, the train and the students still arrived at the debate tournament on time.

explanation: This revision combines the ideas clearly and shows the contrast between lateness and arriving on time. The main trap is “The train reached the station late, the students still arrived at the debate tournament on time.” because it is a comma splice.

prompt:
The school media room allows students to edit interviews, designing posters, and to create short documentaries.
Which revision best corrects the underlined portion?

choices.A: edit interviews, design posters, and create short documentaries
choices.B: edit interviews, designing posters, and creating short documentaries
choices.C: to edit interviews, design posters, and creating short documentaries
choices.D: edit interviews, to design posters, and create short documentaries

explanation: The series needs parallel verbs, and this choice keeps all three in the same form. The main trap is “edit interviews, designing posters, and to create short documentaries” because it mixes verb forms.

prompt:
Some students think strong research writing is mostly about collecting facts. However, effective papers also depend on organization, analysis, and clear explanation.
Which revision of the underlined transition best fits the relationship between the two sentences?

choices.A: However
choices.B: For example
choices.C: Likewise
choices.D: Meanwhile

explanation: The second sentence contrasts with the first sentence’s claim, so “However” is the best transition. The main trap is “Likewise” because the ideas are not similar.

prompt:
After reviewing family feedback for several weeks, a revised dismissal plan was proposed by the principal and office staff.
Which revision best corrects the misplaced modifier in the underlined portion?

choices.A: the principal and office staff proposed a revised dismissal plan
choices.B: a revised dismissal plan, proposed by the principal and office staff
choices.C: the proposal of a revised dismissal plan by the principal and office staff
choices.D: a revised dismissal plan was being proposed by the principal and office staff

explanation: This revision fixes the misplaced modifier by placing the people who reviewed the feedback right after the introductory phrase. The main trap is “a revised dismissal plan, proposed by the principal and office staff” because it creates a fragment, not a complete sentence.

prompt:
A student is ending a speech that argues for adding more refill stations in the school building. The final sentence currently reads: “More refill stations would be nice.”
Which revision would be most effective for persuading the audience?

choices.A: Water bottles can be made from different materials.
choices.B: Adding more refill stations would make it easier for students to stay hydrated and would reduce the use of single-use plastic bottles during the school day.
choices.C: The school building has several hallways and staircases.
choices.D: More refill stations would be placed there.

explanation: This revision is the most persuasive because it gives specific benefits. The main trap is “The school building has several hallways and staircases.” because it does not support the argument.

prompt:
Coach Daniels handed the practice forms to Ava and I before the meet.
Which revision best corrects the underlined word?

choices.A: me
choices.B: mine
choices.C: myself
choices.D: we

explanation: The pronoun follows the preposition “to,” so the objective form “me” is correct. The main trap is “myself” because a reflexive pronoun is not needed here.

prompt:
Jules shouted "Careful!" when the stack of boxes tipped toward the paint table.
Which revision best punctuates the underlined portion?

choices.A: shouted, "Careful!"
choices.B: shouted "Careful"!
choices.C: shouted; "Careful!"
choices.D: shouted: "Careful",

explanation: A comma correctly introduces the quotation, and the exclamation point belongs inside the quotation marks. The main trap is “shouted "Careful"!” because the punctuation is incorrectly placed.

prompt:
We searched the equipment shelf for several minutes. Finally, Nora found the missing extension cord behind the speaker case.
Which transition best replaces the underlined word?

choices.A: Finally
choices.B: For example
choices.C: Instead
choices.D: Similarly

explanation: The word fits because Nora finds the extension cord after searching for several minutes. The main trap is “Instead” because there is no contrast between the two sentences.

prompt:
By the end of the volunteer afternoon, the reading garden had been weeded, the benches had been wiped clean, and the gravel path had been raked smooth again.
Which sentence would best conclude the paragraph?

choices.A: Gravel paths need regular care in outdoor spaces.
choices.B: The benches sit beneath two flowering trees near the fence.
choices.C: As the volunteers stacked their gloves by the gate, the whole garden seemed ready for quiet visitors once more.
choices.D: Many gardens include both flowers and shrubs.

explanation: This sentence gives the paragraph a strong closing image that reflects the result of the work. The main trap is “The benches sit beneath two flowering trees near the fence.” because it adds detail without actually concluding the paragraph.

prompt:
The science club built a display about pollinators for the spring fair. For example, they added labeled diagrams, a short quiz, and a model showing how pollen moves from flower to flower.
Which revision of the underlined transition best fits the relationship between the two sentences?

choices.A: For example
choices.B: However
choices.C: Instead
choices.D: Meanwhile

explanation: The second sentence gives specific examples of the kinds of materials the display included. The main trap is “However” because the two sentences do not contrast.

prompt:
The principal asked Maya, Jordan, and me to present the student proposal at the meeting.
Which revision best corrects the underlined word?

choices.A: me
choices.B: I
choices.C: myself
choices.D: we

explanation: The verb “asked” takes an object, so “me” is correct. The main trap is “I” because the subject form does not work here.

prompt:
The volunteers painted signs for the garden path. Because they wanted visitors to find the herb beds easily.
Which revision best corrects the underlined sentence?

choices.A: Because they wanted visitors to find the herb beds easily.
choices.B: The volunteers painted signs for the garden path because they wanted visitors to find the herb beds easily.
choices.C: Because the herb beds easily wanted visitors to find them.
choices.D: Wanting visitors, the signs were painted easily.

explanation: This revision combines the fragment with the first sentence to create one complete, logical sentence. The main trap is “Because they wanted visitors to find the herb beds easily.” because it remains a fragment.

prompt:
The debate team practiced every afternoon for two weeks. As a result, the speakers felt more prepared during the competition.
Which revision of the underlined transition best fits the relationship between the two sentences?

choices.A: As a result
choices.B: For instance
choices.C: Nevertheless
choices.D: Likewise

explanation: The second sentence shows the consequence of practicing every afternoon. The main trap is “Nevertheless” because the second sentence does not contrast with the first.

prompt:
The art club cleaned the studio, sorted the brushes by size, and stacked the finished canvases against the back wall.
Which sentence would best conclude the paragraph?

choices.A: Paint brushes come in many different shapes.
choices.B: Some canvases were larger than others.
choices.C: By the time the last stool was pushed in, the room looked orderly and ready for the next project.
choices.D: Studios often contain shelves, sinks, and storage bins.

explanation: This sentence provides a clear concluding image that reflects the completed cleanup. The main trap is “Some canvases were larger than others.” because it adds a detail without closing the paragraph.
